coredata.dc:title: Exploring the potential applications of amphiphilic carbon dots based nanocomposite hydrogel in liquid chromatographic separations
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qiaoling
Zhou, Kunming
Liu, Yanjuan
Zhang, Yuefei
Chen, Wei
Tang, Sheng
coredata.dc:description:
Background Due to their excellent stability, low toxicity, flexible modification and adjustable functionality, carbon dots (CDs) have a promising application prospect in the field of chromatographic stationary phases. Hydrogels are new functional polymer materials with three-dimensional network structure that have excellent hydrophilicity, high porosity and unique mechanical properties, which are also good candidate materials for liquid chromatography. Nevertheless, a review of the literature reveals that CDs based nanocomposite hydrogels have not yet been reported as HPLC stationary phases.
Results In this work, amphiphilic CDs with multiple functional groups and polyacrylic acid hydrogel were grafted to the surface of silica gel by an in-situ polymerization method, and a CDs/polyacrylic acid nanocomposite hydrogel stationary phase (CDs/hydrogel@SiO2) was prepared. CDs act as the macroscopic cross-linking agents to form a cross-linked network with polyacrylic acid chains through physical cross-linking by hydrogen bonding and chemical cross-linking by amidation and esterification reactions, which not only improve the swelling property of the hydrogel but also increase its stability. Additionally, the introduction of CDs with multifunctional groups modulates the hydrophilic-hydrophobic balance of the hydrogel that also imparts good hydrophobicity to the composite hydrogel. Through the study of retention mechanism and influencing factors, it is certificate that the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 has mixed-mode chromatographic performance. Furthermore, the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column shows great potential for the determination of organic contaminants in environmental water samples.
Significance This work confirms the potential application of CDs/hydrogel composite for the separation of various samples and provides the possibility of developing CDs based nanocomposite hydrogel in the field of liquid chromatography. Introducing CDs into hydrogel can open up a new way for nanocomposite hydrogels to be used in HPLC, which expands the advance of hydrogel and CDs in separation field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanocomposite hydrogel
Mixed mode chromatography
Stationary phase
HPLC

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reversed phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) separation performance
Determination of organic pollutants in lake water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of CDs@SiO2 (A) and CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 (B). LSCM characterization for CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 (C).
(A) FT-IR spectra, (B) TGA curves, (C) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms and (D) pore diameter distribution curves of SiO2-SH, CDs@SiO2, CDs/hydrogel@SiO2.

(A) Separation of five nucleosides/bases on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column; Effect of H2O content (B), salt concentration (C) and pH (D) on the retention of nucleosides/bases. Mobile phase: (A) ACN/H2O (80/20, v/v); (B) different proportions of ACN/H2O (v/v); (C) ACN/different concentrations of NH4Ac (80/20, v/v); (D) ACN/10 mM NH4Ac (80/20, v/v) at different pH.

(A) The van't Hoff curves fitted for nucleosides/bases. Analytes: 1. thymidine, 2. hypoxanthine, 3. adenosine, 4. adenine, 5. cytidine. (B) Separation of five nucleosides/bases at different temperatures.

Separation of six alkylbenzenes (A), six polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (B), four phthalates (C), and five alkylphenols (D) on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column. Mobile phase: (A) ACN/H2O (20/80, v/v); (B) ACN/H2O (30/70, v/v); (C) ACN/H2O (25/75, v/v); (D) ACN/H2O (20/80, v/v).

head.ce:title: Exploring the potential applications of amphiphilic carbon dots based nanocomposite hydrogel in liquid chromatographic separations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology
Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Linyi University, Shuangling Road, Linyi 276000, Shandong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Linyi University, Shuangling Road, Linyi 276000, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their excellent stability, low toxicity, flexible modification and adjustable functionality, carbon dots (CDs) have a promising application prospect in the field of chromatographic stationary phases. Hydrogels are new functional polymer materials with three-dimensional network structure that have excellent hydrophilicity, high porosity and unique mechanical properties, which are also good candidate materials for liquid chromatography. Nevertheless, a review of the literature reveals that CDs based nanocomposite hydrogels have not yet been reported as HPLC stationary phases.

In this work, amphiphilic CDs with multiple functional groups and polyacrylic acid hydrogel were grafted to the surface of silica gel by an in-situ polymerization method, and a CDs/polyacrylic acid nanocomposite hydrogel stationary phase (CDs/hydrogel@SiO2) was prepared. CDs act as the macroscopic cross-linking agents to form a cross-linked network with polyacrylic acid chains through physical cross-linking by hydrogen bonding and chemical cross-linking by amidation and esterification reactions, which not only improve the swelling property of the hydrogel but also increase its stability. Additionally, the introduction of CDs with multifunctional groups modulates the hydrophilic-hydrophobic balance of the hydrogel that also imparts good hydrophobicity to the composite hydrogel. Through the study of retention mechanism and influencing factors, it is certificate that the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 has mixed-mode chromatographic performance. Furthermore, the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column shows great potential for the determination of organic contaminants in environmental water samples.

This work confirms the potential application of CDs/hydrogel composite for the separation of various samples and provides the possibility of developing CDs based nanocomposite hydrogel in the field of liquid chromatography. Introducing CDs into hydrogel can open up a new way for nanocomposite hydrogels to be used in HPLC, which expands the advance of hydrogel and CDs in separation field.

A novel amphiphilic carbon dots-based nanocomposite hydrogel modified silica stationary phase (CDs/hydrogel@SiO2) was proposed for mixed mode liquid chromatography.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), as an extensively used separation technology, plays an indispensable role in the fields of environment, medicine, food and other fields due to its good selectivity and high efficiency [1–6]. The stationary phase is a key component of HPLC, which determines the separation mode of HPLC and the elution behavior of analytes [7–9]. Currently, mixed-mode chromatographic stationary phase has become the mainstream in the field of chromatographic separation due to its strong separation ability and good selectivity [10–16]. Therefore, the development, design and synthesis of novel mixed-mode chromatographic stationary phases has been one of the hot research spots in analytical science.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been of great interest to researchers for their excellent stability, low toxicity, flexible modification and adjustable functionality [17]. CDs are not only readily available and structurally controllable, but also contain a variety of functional groups that can provide multiple interactions [18]. In recent years, the application of CDs in the field of chromatographic stationary phases has been extensively reported [19–21]. It has been shown that CDs are promising chromatographic separation materials that can improve separation selectivity and efficiency.

Hydrogels are new functional polymer materials with three-dimensional network structure. Due to their remarkable hydrophilicity, high porosity and unique mechanical properties, hydrogels have been widely concerned [22–24]. However, the conventional hydrogels are highly susceptible to swelling, which is difficult to meet the needs of practical applications in liquid chromatography. The above problems can be addressed by combining hydrogels with other functional materials. The incorporation of nanomaterials into the hydrogels networks to improve the performance of hydrogels has been reported [25–27]. Fan et al. introduced metal-organic framework (MOF) into the hydrogel to obtain a nanomaterial-assisted hydrogel composite, which could be applied for mixed-mode liquid chromatography [28]. Besides, the same research group prepared another MOF/hydrogel nanocomposite based mixed mode stationary phase with enhanced separation selectivity as well as excellent stability [29]. Multifunctional nanomaterials usually function as functional cores to endow the nanocomposite hydrogels with special properties such as anti-swelling and good plasticity [30]. A review of the literature reveals that CDs based nanocomposite hydrogel modified silica gel has not been previously reported as a HPLC stationary phase.

In this work, amphiphilic CDs with multiple functional groups and polyacrylic acid hydrogel were grafted to the surface of silica gel by the in-situ polymerization method, and a CDs/polyacrylic acid nanocomposite hydrogel stationary phase (CDs/hydrogel@SiO2) was prepared. As the macroscopic crosslinking agents, CDs form cross-linking networks with polyacrylic acid chains through physical cross-linking of hydrogen bonds and chemical cross-linking of amide and ester bonds in amidation and esterification reactions. CDs not only improve the swelling property of hydrogel, but also increase the stability of hydrogel. In addition, CDs with multiple functional groups are introduced to modulate the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of the hydrogel and make the composite hydrogel have good swelling resistance. The prepared CDs/hydrogel composite was used for separating various hydrophilic and hydrophobic compounds, including nucleosides/bases, alkylbenzenes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, phthalates, etc., and its hydrophilic selectivity was compared with that of CDs@SiO2. Finally, the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column was applied to determine organic pollutants in an environmental water sample.

Amphiphilic CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method [31]. During the synthesis process, 2.0 g of anhydrous citric acid and 1.0 g of 1,8-octylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and ultrasonically blended for 10 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a sealed Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The reaction solution was cooled to room temperature, and then placed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 3 days, followed by lyophilization to obtain a solid product.

The synthesis process of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 is depicted in Fig. 1 . Briefly, 2.25 g of SiO2-SH, 0.3 g of acrylic acid and 0.15 g of N,N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) were added into 50 mL of methanol, and then 0.08 g of CDs and 0.05 g of 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) were added. The above mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 24 h. After the reaction was completed, the obtained CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 was washed with methanol and deionized water, respectively, and finally vacuum-dried.

In order to investigate the significant role of composite hydrogel, CDs@SiO2 and hydrogel@SiO2 were prepared for comparative experiments. Briefly, 2.25 g of SiO2-SH, 0.15 g of N,N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) were added into 50 mL of methanol, and then 0.08 g of CDs or 0.3 g of acrylic acid and 0.05 g of 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) were added and stirred at 80 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, the resulting CDs@SiO2 or hydrogel@SiO2 stationary phase was washed and dried by the same way as described above.
The method of filling an empty HPLC column (150 × 4.6 mm) with CDs/hydrogel@SiO2, CDs@SiO2 or hydrogel@SiO2 was described in the Electronic Supplementary Materials.

Firstly, the morphology of CDs@SiO2 and CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Fig. 2 A and 2B). Based on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs, it can be seen that the bright blue fluorescence of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 by laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) imaging confirmed the successful preparation of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 (Fig. 2C). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of SiO2-SH, CDs@SiO2 and CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 are displayed in Fig. 3 A. The peak at 1537 cm−1 is belonged to the bending vibration of N–H in CDs. The characteristic peak at 1729 cm−1 is caused by CO of the hydrogel. Compared with SiO2-SH, the appearance of new absorption peaks indicates the successful preparation of CDs@SiO2 and CDs/hydrogel@SiO2. Thermogravimetric analysis was performed to measure the weight loss of three stationary phases in the temperature range from 30 to 800 °C (Fig. 3B). The total weight reduction of the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 material is 14.24%, which is larger than that of SiO2-SH (6.64%) and CDs@SiO2 (9.15%), proving that the CDs and CDs/hydrogel were successfully attached to the surface of SiO2-SH. The specific surface area and porosity of SiO2-SH, CDs@SiO2, CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 were assessed (Fig. 3C and 3D), and the corresponding physical parameters of three stationary phases are shown in Table S1. Compared with SiO2-SH, the surface area of CDs@SiO2 changes little since CDs are also porous carbon nanomaterials, while the specific surface area of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 decreases from 244.5 to 214.2 m2 g−1, which is due to that the graft of CDs/hydrogel reduces the pores of SiO2. This can also be seen in the decrease in total pore volume from 0.79 to 0.66 cm3 g−1. The contents of C, N and H of SiO2-SH, CDs@SiO2 and CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 increase successively as shown in Table S1, while the contents of C, N and H of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 are 8.7%, 0.7% and 1.9%, respectively. From these results, it can be concluded that CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2 were successfully prepared.

In this section, five nucleosides/bases were used as probes to investigate the HILIC retention behavior. Hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups in CDs-based nanocomposite hydrogel can form hydrophilic effect and hydrogen bonding interaction with polar compounds. As shown in Fig. 4 A, a mixture of thymidine, hypoxanthine, adenosine, adenine and cytidine was successfully separated on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column. Importantly, the elution order of the five nucleosides/bases is inconsistent with the increasing order of their polarity (Table S2), suggesting that hydrophilic effect is not the only separation mechanism, and that π-π and hydrogen bond interactions may also be involved in retention. In order to verify the significant roles of hydrogel and amphiphilic CDs in the stationary phase, CDs@SiO2 and hydrogel@SiO2 were selected as comparisons for the separation of nucleosides/bases. As shown in Fig. S1, the CDs@SiO2 and hydrogel@SiO2 column could not achieve a baseline separation of the five hydrophilic compounds under optimal conditions, and the highest column efficiency of the CDs@SiO2 (22785 N m−1) and hydrogel@SiO2 column (7584 N m−1) was lower than that of the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column (30008 N m−1, Table S3). The above results indicate that the synergistic effect of hydrogel and amphiphilic CDs is necessary for the superior hydrophilic separation efficiency of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column. Compared with the HILIC-XAmide column, the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column exhibited more favorable separation selectivity for these five nucleosides/bases. In addition, an effective separation of five nucleosides/bases on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column was realized in a shorter time. However, it is slightly unfortunate that the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column is weaker than the HILIC-XAmide column in terms of column efficiency. The development of new chromatographic columns comparable to the commercial columns has always been the eternal pursuit of chromatographers. Therefore, developing chromatographic columns with high column efficiency will be one of the main focuses of our research work in the future.

As shown in Fig. 4B, the influence changes in mobile phase with aqueous phase of 10–30% on the retention were investigated to discuss the interaction mechanism. As the increase of the H2O content, the retention time of the five analytes decreased, exhibiting significant HILIC retention characteristics. The retention mechanism of HILIC is based on the selective distribution of analytes between the adhesive water layer on the surface of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 and the mobile phase with a high ACN content. A higher content of organic phase not only drives the hydrophilic analytes into the water layer attached to the stationary phase surface, but also potentially increases the volume of the adhering water layer, thus enhancing the retention of analytes [32]. In order to understand the separation mechanism, a HILIC adsorption and partition mechanism model was established. The retention in adsorption mechanism can be expressed by Eq. (1), and the partition mechanism can be expressed by Eq. (2) [33] (see Electronic Supplementary Materials).

For the adsorption mechanism, the relationship between log k and log X B (mole fraction of water) follows a linear regression (Fig. S2A), while for the partition mechanism, the relationship between log k and φ (volume fraction of water) follows a linear regression (Fig. S2B). The determination coefficients of the adsorption and partitioning models fitted for Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively, are shown in Table S4. The R 2 adsorption for most analytes is greater than R 2 partition, but R 2 partition for thymidine is greater than R 2 adsorption, indicating that adsorption and partition mechanisms are both involved in the retention of analytes on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column [34].

The salt concentration and pH are considerable factors for chromatographic separation. It can be seen that the effect of salt concentration on the analyte retention is complex (Fig. 4C). The retention of nucleosides/bases fluctuates with changes in salt concentration. The increase of buffer concentration will thicken the enriched water layer on the stationary phase surface, which may be contribute to the increase of hydrophilic retention. The decrease in retention may be result from the fact that the increased salt concentration weakens the electrostatic interaction. The effect of mobile phase pH on the retention time of five analytes was also studied. As shown in Fig. 4D, the retention of five nucleosides/bases declines slightly with the decreases of pH. As the pH decreases, the protonation degree of the analytes as well as the amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 will be gradually deepened, causing both analytes and stationary phase to be positively charged, increasing the electrostatic repulsion force, and weakening the analyte retention.

Column temperature has important effects on solute diffusion coefficient, mobile phase viscosity and solute transfer between two phases. Normally, the relationship between the retention factor and column temperature can be described by Eq. (3) (see Electronic Supplementary Materials). For researching the retention behavior of analytes on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 stationary phase from the perspective of thermodynamics, nucleosides/bases were selected as probes for separation in the range of 25–45 °C. As shown in Fig. 5 , the retention time of the five analytes decreases gradually with the increase of temperature, indicating that the separation process of analytes on the column is an exothermic process. Meanwhile, the van't Hoff curves for adenosine, adenine and cytidine show a linear relationship (Table S5), indicating that the interaction mechanism remains basically unchanged during the process. The non-linear van't Hoff curves for thymidine and hypoxanthine can be attributed to the multiple interactions in the separation process and multiple forms of analytes in the solution [35]. In short, this nonlinear relationship can be explained by the fact that the separation is predominantly based on multiple retention mechanisms, including hydrophilic, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic and other interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversed phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) separation performance
ce:section.ce:para: The alkyl chains grafted on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 material can provide hydrophobic interaction with the analytes, so the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 has the potential for RPLC. As can be seen from Fig. 6A, six alkylbenzenes were separated. With the increase of the content of ACN, the analyte retention decreases (Fig. S3A), showing typical RPLC characteristics. The peak order of alkylbenzenes is benzene < toluene < ethylbenzene < propylbenzene < butylbenzene < phenylpentane, which is consistent with their hydrophobic ordering (Table S6), suggesting that the separation of alkylbenzenes is based on the hydrophobic interaction. In order to explore the effect of organic phase content on retention, a mechanism model of hydrophobic interaction in RPLC model is established as Eq. (4) [36] (see Electronic Supplementary Materials). The fitted results for the alkylbenzenes (Fig. S3B and Table S7) also indicate that the separation of the hydrophobic analytes mainly depends on the hydrophobic interaction.

Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons are toxic organic pollutants with potential health risks, which are widely distributed in the environment. As shown in Fig. 6B, six polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons were separated, and the elution sequence was naphthalene < fluorene < phenanthrene < fluoranthene < pyrene <1,2-benzenanthrene. The elution sequence is consistent with the increase of the hydrophobic coefficient (Log P, Table S8) and π electron conjugate system of the analytes, indicating that the separation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons depends on the hydrophobicity and π-π interaction.

Phthalates are used in a wide range of consumer products, such as food packaging, which have been shown to interfere with the hormone production mechanism and disrupt the endocrine system. Phthalates were therefore chosen to investigate the potential application of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column. As shown in Fig. 6 C, a baseline separation of four phthalates was achieved. The elution order of phthalates does not exactly follow the incremental order of Log P (Table S9), indicating that hydrophobic effect is not the only separation mechanism. The separation of phthalates can be attributed to multiple interactions.

Herein, the separation effect of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column for alkylphenols was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6D, the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column shows good separation effect for five alkylphenols. However, the elution sequence of the five alkylphenols is inconsistent with the order of their hydrophobic coefficients (Table S10). This may be attribute to the synergistic effect of hydrophobic and hydrogen bonding interactions. It is further indicated that CDs can confer good hydrophobic selectivity of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2.

For comparisons, CDs@SiO2, hydrogel@SiO2 and C18 column were selected for the separation of these hydrophobic analytes to reveal the potential of the proposed CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column (Fig. S4-S7). Under optimal conditions, most of the hydrophobic analytes such as alkylbenzenes, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and phthalates were separated in less time on the CDs@SiO2 column than on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column, and the hydrogel@SiO2 column showed poor separation efficiency for hydrophobic substances. The above results indicate that amphiphilic CDs play a significant role in the separation of hydrophobic substances, and meanwhile, the coordination of amphiphilic CDs and hydrogels can ensure the fast and efficient separation on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column. Compared with the commercial C18 column, the column efficiency of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 is not very satisfactory, which means that there is still work to be done to achieve commercialization. However, the separation of four types of hydrophobic analytes can be realized on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column with fewer organic phases. The amount of organic phase used on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column is half or less than that on the C18 column. In other words, CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column can reduce experimental costs and facilitate the development of green chromatography, which has potentially valuable applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of organic pollutants in lake water
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column was applied to detect alkylbenzenes from lake water sample to investigate its practical application potential. Fig. S8 shows the chromatograms of spiked sample, standard solution and lake water sample. The sample substrate has no significant interference effect on the detection of alkylbenzenes. This result additionally declares the enormous potential of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column in analyzing environmental contaminants in real samples.

In this study, a novel stationary phase preparation strategy by grafting of CDs/polyacrylic acid nanocomposite hydrogel onto the silica surface via in-situ polymerization using CDs as the macroscopic cross-linking agents was proposed. CDs formed cross-linking networks with polyacrylic acid chains through physical and chemical cross-linking. Five nucleosides/bases, six alkylbenzenes, six polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, four phthalates and five alkylphenols were well separated on the CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 column. The separation mechanism can be inferred as hydrophilic, hydrophobic, hydrogen bonding, π-π and electrostatic interactions, etc. In addition, the successful analysis of real sample further reveals the great potential of CDs/hydrogel@SiO2 stationary phase for HPLC applications. Overall, this work not only proposes a new mixed-mode stationary phase candidate material, but also expands the potential applications of CDs-based nanocomposite hydrogels in separation science.

Qiaoling Liu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Kunming Zhou: Data curation, Investigation, Validation. Yanjuan Liu: Formal analysis, Resources, Visualization. Yuefei Zhang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Wei Chen: Resources, Supervision. Sheng Tang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere thanks to the support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21906124), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (No. 2017CFB220) and Graduate Innovative Fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (No. CX2023135).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2024.342445.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantification and carbon and nitrogen isotopic measurements of heme B in environmental samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Isaji, N.O. Ogawa, Y. Takano, N. Ohkouchi, Quantification and carbon and nitrogen isotopic measurements of heme B in environmental samples, Anal. Chem. 92 (2020) 11213-11222.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The secret of reversed-phase/weak cation exchange retention mechanisms in mixed-mode liquid chromatography applied for small drug molecule analysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances and applications of graphene-based extraction materials in food safety

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.D. Hou, S. Tang, J. Wang, Recent advances and applications of graphene-based extraction materials in food safety, Trends Anal. Chem. 119 (2019) 115603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of neotame in various foods by high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with ultraviolet and mass spectrometric detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.A. Zhu, Y.H. Chen, L.L. Yu, C.Y. Hou, X.Q. Qiao, T.T. Wang, Determination of neotame in various foods by high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with ultraviolet and mass spectrometric detection, Food Chem. 416 (2023) 135863.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of graphene-based nanoadsorbent materials for environmental contaminants detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Huang, X.Q. Tang, K.X. Luo, Y. Wu, X.D. Hou, S. Tang, An overview of graphene-based nanoadsorbent materials for environmental contaminants detection, Trends Anal. Chem. 139 (2021) 116255.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ingenious introduction of aminopropylimidazole to tune the hydrophobic selectivity of dodecyl-bonded stationary phase for environmental organic pollutants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wu, P.P. Cao, Y.H. Jiang, Y.J. Liu, Y.F. Zhang, W. Chen, Z.W. Bai, S. Tang, Ingenious introduction of aminopropylimidazole to tune the hydrophobic selectivity of dodecyl-bonded stationary phase for environmental organic pollutants, Microchem. J. 182 (2022) 107933.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances of innovative and high-efficiency stationary phases for chromatographic separations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wu, N. Zhang, K.X. Luo, Y.J. Liu, Z.W. Bai, S. Tang, Recent advances of innovative and high-efficiency stationary phases for chromatographic separations, Trends Anal. Chem. 153 (2022) 116647.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chromatographic separation performance of silica microspheres surface-modified with triazine-containing imine-linked covalent organic frameworks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.Y. Long, Y.H. Jiang, Y.J. Liu, Y.F. Zhang, W. Chen, S. Tang, Chromatographic separation performance of silica microspheres surface-modified with triazine-containing imine-linked covalent organic frameworks, Talanta 260 (2023) 124589.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Octadecylamine and glucose-coderived hydrophobic carbon dots-modified porous silica for chromatographic separation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, N. Yuan, D.N. Jiang, Q. Lei, B. Liu, W.Y. Tang, K.H. Row, H.D. Qiu, Octadecylamine and glucose-coderived hydrophobic carbon dots-modified porous silica for chromatographic separation, Chin. Chem. Lett. 32 (2021) 3398-3401.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chitosan/polyacrylic acid/octadecene double-crosslinked network hydrogel functionalized porous silica microspheres for multimode liquid chromatographic separation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophobic/hydrophilic separation performance evaluation of a mixed-mode ionic liquid embedded stearyl thioglycolate functionalized silica stationary phase
